coredata.dc:title: Electrochemiluminescence resonance energy transfer system fabricated by quantum state complexes for cardiac troponin I detection
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Zhanglei
Liu, Shanghua
Li, Yueyuan
Tang, Feng
Zhao, Zengdian
Liu, Qing
Li, Yueyun
Wei, Qin
coredata.dc:description: Electrochemiluminescence resonance energy transfer (ECL-RET) as an energy transfer model is used to explain signal quenching or enhancement phenomena. Establishing this model requires specific conditions including efficient electrochemiluminescence (ECL) emitters and energy donors with highly overlapping spectra and sufficient distance to complete the energy transfer. In this work, a novel ECL-based biosensor was proposed by utilizing graphene quantum dots (GQDs)-coupled gold nanoclusters (Au NCs) nanocomposites supported on a well-ordered Co3O4 nanoarrays (NAs) sensing substrate as signal labels. The as-synthesized Au NCs with controllable size by using glutathione as ligand showed stable ECL signal in triethylamine (TEA) aqueous solution. After coupling with GQDs, the obtained quantum state complexes (Au NCs-GQDs) are compact in space relation, and benefiting from resonance energy transfer and synergetic effect, thus producing higher ECL response. The prepared Co3O4 NAs exhibit consistent density orientation, which can not only work as an ideal substrate for capturing more immune molecules, but also act as a conductor to accelerate electron transport. This rational designed biosensor has been constructed for detecting cardiac troponin I (cTnI), a pivotal biomarker of acute myocardial infarction (AMI). Under optimal conditions, the biosensor showed a good ECL response of cTnI with a satisfactory linear range from 500 fg/mL to 20 ng/mL and an ultralow limit of detection of 354.2 fg/mL. With the excellent repeatability and stability, this method provides a clue for the expansion of the enhance-type ECL-RET model.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrochemiluminescence resonance energy transfer
Donor-accepter pairs
Gold nanoclusters
Graphene quantum dots
Co3O4 nanoarrays
Biosensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Parameters of the ECL measurement method
Characterization of Co(OH)F NAs, Co3O4 NAs, Au NCs and GQDs
Increased electroactive surface area of Co3O4 NAs
Characterization for the fabrication of biosensor

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) XRD patterns of Co(OH)F NAs and Co3O4 NAs. (B) XPS of Co3O4 NAs. High-resolution XPS spectra of (C) Co 2p and (D) O 1s for Co3O4 NAs.
SEM images of (A) Co(OH)F NAs and (B) Co3O4 NAs. HRTEM images of (C) GQDs and (D) Au NCs.

(A) The CV curve in different scan rates. (B) UV–vis absorption spectrum of Au NCs (orange line) and emission PL spectrum of Au NCs (black line), GQDs (blue line) and Au NCs-GQDs (red line). (C) ECL intensity-times curves of Au NCs (black line), GQDs (blue line) and Au NCs-GQDs (red line). (D) The stepwise characterization of the proposed biosensor by the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) characterization. (a) NH2-Co3O4/GCE, (b) Bare GCE, (c) NH2-Co3O4-Ab1/GCE, (d) NH2-Co3O4-Ab1-BSA/GCE, (e) NH2-Co3O4-Ab1-BSA-cTnI/GCE, (f) NH2-Co3O4-Ab1-BSA-cTnI-Au NCs-GQDs-Ab2/GCE (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).

(A) ECL intensity-times curves, and (B) Calibration curve of the biosensor 500 fg/mL, 5 pg/mL, 50 pg/mL, 500 pg/mL, 1 ng/mL, 5 ng/mL, 10 ng/mL, 20 ng/mL. (C) Stability of the biosensor in thirty-two scans. (D) Specificity of the proposed ECL biosensor. (E) Reproducibility of six different electrodes in same batches. (F) Reproducibility of six electrodes in different batches. Error bars, SD, n = 5.
Preparation Process of (A) Au NCs-GQDs-Ab2 bioconjugate and (B) the proposed ECL biosensor for cTnI detection.

head.ce:title: Electrochemiluminescence resonance energy transfer system fabricated by quantum state complexes for cardiac troponin I detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shandong University of Technology, Zibo, 255049, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shandong University of Technology, Zibo255049, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Collaborative Innovation Center for Green Chemical Manufacturing and Accurate Detection, Key Laboratory of Interfacial Reaction & Sensing Analysis in Universities of Shandong, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, University of Jinan, Jinan, 250022, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Collaborative Innovation Center for Green Chemical Manufacturing and Accurate Detection
Key Laboratory of Interfacial Reaction & Sensing Analysis in Universities of Shandong
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Collaborative Innovation Center for Green Chemical Manufacturing and Accurate Detection, Key Laboratory of Interfacial Reaction & Sensing Analysis in Universities of Shandong, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, University of Jinan, Jinan250022, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Electrochemiluminescence resonance energy transfer (ECL-RET) as an energy transfer model is used to explain signal quenching or enhancement phenomena. Establishing this model requires specific conditions including efficient electrochemiluminescence (ECL) emitters and energy donors with highly overlapping spectra and sufficient distance to complete the energy transfer. In this work, a novel ECL-based biosensor was proposed by utilizing graphene quantum dots (GQDs)-coupled gold nanoclusters (Au NCs) nanocomposites supported on a well-ordered Co3O4 nanoarrays (NAs) sensing substrate as signal labels. The as-synthesized Au NCs with controllable size by using glutathione as ligand showed stable ECL signal in triethylamine (TEA) aqueous solution. After coupling with GQDs, the obtained quantum state complexes (Au NCs-GQDs) are compact in space relation, and benefiting from resonance energy transfer and synergetic effect, thus producing higher ECL response. The prepared Co3O4 NAs exhibit consistent density orientation, which can not only work as an ideal substrate for capturing more immune molecules, but also act as a conductor to accelerate electron transport. This rational designed biosensor has been constructed for detecting cardiac troponin I (cTnI), a pivotal biomarker of acute myocardial infarction (AMI). Under optimal conditions, the biosensor showed a good ECL response of cTnI with a satisfactory linear range from 500 fg/mL to 20 ng/mL and an ultralow limit of detection of 354.2 fg/mL. With the excellent repeatability and stability, this method provides a clue for the expansion of the enhance-type ECL-RET model.

ce:section.ce:para: The detection of low-abundance biomarkers is of great value for the early diagnosis and prevention of diseases [1,2]. Protein, deoxyribonucleic acid and ribonucleic acid-types biomarkers can be expediently detected by modern analysis equipment, such as electrophoresis, chromatography, nuclear magnetism and et al. [3–5]. Analogously, the electrochemical method has become a significant strategy for trace analysis because its good controllability and high signal to noisy [6]. As an important branch of electrochemistry, ECL integrates the advantages of both electrochemistry and chemiluminescence technologies, and has become one of the most common techniques in biological analysis [7,8]. With the continuous exploration of biosensor accuracy, many signal amplification strategies have been developed and applied, such as coreactant acceleration, aggregation-induced emission, resonance energy transfer, metal surface energy transfer and so on [9–12].

Currently, ECL-RET is often employed to explain ECL quenching or enhancement phenomena, but the conditions for its existence are harsh [13]. Whether RET can occur depends on the overlapping degree between the emission spectrum of donor and the absorption spectrum of accepter, the distance of donor-accepter (D-A) pairs, and the relative dipole orientation of the energy transfer [14,15]. Thus, it would be inappropriate to classify signal amplification or quenching with the RET without meeting these conditions. On the other hand, there are various requirements for appropriate D-A pairs in terms of quantum yield, extinction coefficient, water solubility, anti-interference and so on [16,17]. Therefore, it is difficult to find efficient, non-toxic and suitable D-A pairs to improve the detection sensitivity of ECL-RET system.

The size of metal nanoclusters is usually smaller than two nanometers, which is close to the Fermi wavelength of the electron [18]. The continuous state density is decomposed into discrete energy levels, resulting in different optical, electrical and chemical properties compared with nanoparticles [19,20]. Moreover, there are abundant functional groups on the surface of Au NCs synthesized with glutathione as ligand, which provides convenience for its subsequent functionalization [19]. There are a large number of chemical functional sites on the sides and edges of GQDs making it easy to combine with a variety of nanomaterials to produce nano-hybrids with different properties [21]. In addition, GQDs have the merits of good fluorescence, low toxicity and biocompatibility, which thus can be used as a fluorescent label, quenching agent, energy or electronic donor. Besides, the properties of excitation constraint and quantum size effect which makes it have optical activity, electrical conductivity and chemical inertia. All of these properties make them an excellent material for biosensors signal label [22,23].

Electrons of Au NCs can produce transition in their metal nucleus, including the internal electronic transition between d orbital and sp orbital, and the adjacent electronic transition between the highest occupied orbital and the lowest unoccupied orbital of sp orbital, so it has an obvious quantum size effect [10]. This means that when the size of the nanoclusters decreases, the distance between discrete states in each orbit also increases, leading to the spectral shift of the emission peak [24]. Benefiting from this property, nanoclusters are well suited to be D-A pairs in RET because their optical properties can be regulated via controllable synthesis in size. At the same time, the emission spectrum of GQDs overlaps with the absorption spectrum of Au NCs [18,25]. Accordingly, by bridging Au NCs with GQDs through 1-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) and N-Hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), an ECL signal label based on ECL-RET strategy was successfully conducted. The obtained quantum state complexes controlled the D-A pairs distance within 10 nm, which allowed them to adhere strictly to the RET principle.

Thanks to its unique properties, nanoarrays have been widely used in the study of electrochemistry. Their unique structure is especially conducive to the directional electron transport, which speeds up the transfer of electrons from the electrode to the signal label. Undoubtedly, such structure is facilitated to improve the performance of the biosensor and expanding the detection limit of biosensor [26,27]. Moreover, nanoarrays have larger specific surface area, to provides more active sites and better catalytic ability [27,28].

In this work, an ECL signal label was fabricated using glutathione-synthesized Au NCs as energy acceptor and GQDs as energy donor. The capture antibody (Ab1) was incubated on the Co3O4 NAs, subsequently Ab1 captured the antigen. Then a sandwich immune structure was obtained by the antigen fixed the bioconjugate labeled complex of the detection antibody (Ab2) [29]. Benefiting from the excellent surface electrical activity of the Co3O4 NAs and the ECL-RET effect between Au NCs and GQDs, the biological cognition between Au NCs-GQDs-Ab2 and the target antigen is effectively converted into ECL signal, thus enabling the efficient perception of antigens at different concentrations [30]. In addition, AMI is a disease that endangers human life. Because of its specificity and long detection window, cTnI is an ideal biomarker for AMI [31]. The designed biosensor can be used for the ultra-sensitive detection of cTnI, which proves the accuracy and practicality of the biosensor [32,33].
The reagents, materials and apparatus involved in this work are supplemented in the supporting information.
The preparation method of Co(OH)F NAs, Co3O4 NAs, Au NCs, GO and GQDs are supplemented in the supporting information.

As shown in Scheme 1 A, 100 μL of EDC (20 mg/mL), 100 μL of NHS (20 mg/mL) and 1 mL of Au NCs (0.158 mg/mL, Au) were gently mixed at room temperature for 30 min. Then, 1 mL of GQDs solution be added to the above solution, the Au NCs-GQDs were prepared after stirring for 1 h [20]. Following, the Au NCs-GQDs (0.079 mg/mL, Au) mixed with 1 mL of Ab2 (200 μg/mL), and incubated at 4 °C for 12 h. The final mixture was centrifuged and redispersed 100 μL of BSA (1%) in the phosphate buffered saline (pH 7.38, PBS) for 30 min to block the residual binding sites. The Au NCs-GQDs-Ab2 was washed three times with PBS, and then the Au NCs-GQDs-Ab2 was dispersed into 1 mL of PBS and stored at 4 °C for further use.

3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES) of 5% concentration was prepared with ethanol as solvent, 10 mg of Co3O4 NAs was immersed in the prepared solution, and the amino group was formed on the surface at 4 °C for 12 h [29].

As shown in Scheme 1B, the glassy carbon electrode (GCE) was carefully polished by alumina powder, then rinsed several times with ultra-pure water and ethanol. Next, drop 10 μL of NH2-Co3O4 NAs (1 mg/mL) onto the surface of bare GCE, after which 10 μL of Ab1 (100 μg/mL) was modified onto NH2-Co3O4 NAs/GCE and incubated for 2 h at 4 °C. Then, gently rinsed the surface of NH2-Co3O4 NAs-Ab1/GCE to remove unsecured Ab1, and blocked remaining activated amidogen sites for 1 h by 1% of BSA (pH 7.38). After incubated 10 μL of cTnI antigen successfully, covered NH2-Co3O4 NAs-Ab1-Ag/GCE with 10 μL of Au NCs/GQDs-Ab2 to assemble the sandwich-type biosensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Parameters of the ECL measurement method
ce:section.ce:para: All ECL data were measured by the MPI-E electrochemiluminescence analysis system in 15 mL of TEA containing KNO3 (0.1 M), wherein the voltage of the photomultiplier was set at 800 V, the cyclic voltampere test voltage was from 0.6 to 1.35 V, and the scanning rate was 25 mv/s.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of Co(OH)F NAs, Co3O4 NAs, Au NCs and GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Effective characterization methods were used to characterize the details of the as-prepared material. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern in Fig. 1 A shows the crystal structure of Co(OH)F NAs and Co3O4 NAs. Among them, there are (110), (310), (111), (211), (221), (420) lattice planes of Co(OH)F NAs at 20.84°, 32.32°, 35.57°, 38.78°, 51.95°, 53.78° respectively, and the curve of Co3O4 NAs shows that there are (111), (220), (311), (400), (511), (440) lattice planes at 19.00°, 31.27°, 36.85°, 44.81°, 59.36°, 65.24°, from which all the diffraction peaks are well matched, indicating that both of them have been successfully prepared [27,34]. In Fig. 2 A, the scanning electron microscope (SEM) image shows a regular nanoarray composed of cylindrical structures. After calcination, the smooth cylindrical shape becomes a packing-structure in Fig. 2B, which is further confirmed by Figure S1 in supporting information. In Figure S2, the atomic ratio of Co, O elements in the image of the energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) is close to 3:4. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) image of Co3O4 NAs is shown in Fig. 1B, the corresponding peaks of Co (2p) and O (1 s) can all be found. Fig. 1C shows the high-resolution XPS spectra of Co 2p for Co3O4 NAs, the binding energy peaks at 779.88 and 794.85 eV belong to Co2+, while the binding energy peaks at 781.37 and 796.42 eV belong to Co3+ [35]. Fig. 1D shows the high-resolution O1 s spectrum. Peak at 592.98 eV can be indexed to Co-O species, while peaks at 530.73 and 531.92 eV can be ascribed to oxygen species in Co3O4 NAs, demonstrating the formation of the Co3O4 NAs phase [35]. In addition, In Figure S3, the Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) image demonstrated that Co3O4 NAs was successfully amino functionalized, after amino functionalization, the carboxyl on the surface of the antibody can react with the amidogen on the surface of Co3O4 NRs to immobilize the antibody [29].

The high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) provides a method for observing the morphologic structure of materials with a much higher resolution. As shown in Fig. 2C, there are flaky two-dimensional structures with a diameter of about 5 nm, and the crystal lattice distance is measured at 0.335 nm, which corresponded crystal face is (003) plane of graphene, proving that GQDs was successfully prepared. In Fig. 2D, Au NCs are evenly distributed in the images, according to HRTEM image, the crystal lattice distance is measured at 0.234 nm and 0.204 nm, which corresponded crystal face are (111) and (200) planes of Au NCs. The diameter range of the as-prepared Au NCs is about 2.25 ± 1.25 nm which can be observed in Fig. 2D.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Increased electroactive surface area of Co3O4 NAs
ce:section.ce:para:
In order to explore the surface electrical activity of Co3O4 NAs, using [Fe(CN)6]4‑/3− (5 mM) as the electrochemical probe, the cyclic voltammetry (CV) was used to conduct electrochemical scanning of the GCEs modified with Co3O4 NAs. According to Randles-Ševčík equation [36]:
I p = 2.69 × 105AD1/2 n 3/2 v 1/2 c,
where the I p is the peak value of the restore current during CV scanning, A is the electroactive surface area (mm2) of the material, D is the diffusion coefficient of [Fe(CN)6]4‑/3− (6.70 ± 0.02 × 10−6 cm2/s) at 25 °C. n is the number of electrons transferred (n = 1) in this reaction. v is the scanning rate of CV. c is the concentration of the electrochemical probe. As shown in Fig. 3 A, the image displayed the current curves obtained at different scanning rates, and the regression curve corresponding to the peak of reduction current, the regression equation is I = 2.09×v 1/2–9.62 × 10−7 (R2 = 0.997). According to the above results, the calculated electroactive surface area is 22.44 mm2, compared with the bare GCE (12.56 mm2), the electroactive surface area of the modified electrode is significantly increased.

To study the signal enhancement pattern of RET system, Au NCs, GQDs and Au NCs-GQDs were prepared and characterized by the fluorescence spectrophotometer (FS) in Fig. 3B, the emission peak of GQDs (blue line) is around 480 nm, which is consistent with the observed green light (Figure S4B). The emission peak of Au NCs (black line) is around 610 nm, and the emitted light is orange light (Figure S4A). After isopyknic mixing of Au NCs and GQDs, the prepared Au NCs-GQDs (red line) show stronger PL emission capability (Figure S4C), the emission peak of Au NCs-GQDs is around 530 nm, and the emission peak of it is blue-shifted by 80 nm compared to the emission spectrum of Au NCs, which means the energy of an emitter is increased [37]. Moreover, the emission spectrum of GQDs overlaps that of the UV–vis absorption spectrum of Au NCs (orange line), and the results prove that RET exists between Au NCs and GQDs. In addition, the electrochemical behavior of Au NCs-GQDs was studied in the case of triethylamine (TEA, 0.5 M) as the coreactant. ECL behavior was characterized in PBS (pH 7.38). GQDs, Au NCs and Au NCs-GQDs were modified to the bare glassy carbon electrode, respectively. The results after representation are shown in Fig. 3C. In a stable single ECL cycle, the ECL emission intensity of Au NCs-GQDs is about 2.5 times that of Au NCs. Compared with Au NCs and Au NCs-GQDs, the modified GQDs has almost no ECL behavior. It is proved that the Au NCs-GQDs can occur RET in the system with TEA as the coreactant, which greatly enhances the signal intensity.

The occurrence of ECL - RET may be because the fact that GQDs is very close to Au NCs in space, the energy of GQDs that will be dissipated by molecular vibrations is transferred to Au NCs. In the anodic ECL reaction, Au NCs gain energy to form high energy particles (HEPs), which release more energy outward to obtain higher ECL signal [19,20,38]. The possible mechanisms are as follows:
Au NCs - e → Au NCs·+
TEA - e → TEA·+
Au NCs·+ + TEA·+ → Au NCs* +TEAoxidate
GQDs + e → GQDs·−
GQDs·− + H+ → GQDs·
GQDs· + Au NCs* → [Au NCs*]HEP + GQDs
[Au NCs*]HEP → Au NCs + more hν.

Profit from the RET effect between Au NCs and GQDs, the ECL signal intensity of the quantum complex was greatly increased, which helps to increase the range of analyzable concentrations. By assembling specific sandwich structures, the number of antigens can be reflected by the number of Au NCs-GQDs quantum complexes. The number of antigens can be calculated by detecting the signal strength generated by the quantum complex.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization for the fabrication of biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: The success of stepwise modification was proved by electrochemical characterization, the electrochemical impedance spectrum (EIS) was used to measure the resistance value of the electrode under different modification degrees in [Fe(CN)6]4−/3− (2.5 mM) solution containing KCl (0.1 M), and the scan frequency is 0.1 to 105 Hz. As shown in Fig. 3D, compared with bare GCE (curve b), the semicircle of NH2-Co3O4/GCE (curve a) curve is relatively smaller than that of bare GCE curve, indicating that the resistance value slightly decreases, proving that NH2-Co3O4/GCE has excellent conductivity, this is because the Co3O4 NAs act as electron transport channels, speeding up the transfer of electrons [39,40]. After modifying the electrode surface with NH2-Co3O4 NAs, Ab1, BSA, cTnI and Au NCs-GQDs-Ab2 were successively modified to the electrode surface. With the increase of the degree of electrode modification, the semicircle of these curves (curve c, d, e, f) increases gradually. This is because of the inefficient conductivity of the protein, which leads to the increase of the resistance value of the modified electrode, and indicating that layer upon layer modification of the biosensor was successful.

Vital step in the experiment is to optimize the experimental conditions. To achieve this, we constructed biosensor with 1 ng/mL of cTnI, and discuss the pH of the buffer, the concentration of TEA and the modified concentration of Co3O4 NAs. As shown in Figure S5A, ECL intensity gradually increases with the increase of pH value, but the amplitude of increase gradually slows down, in order to obtain higher ECL signal and maintain the activity of biological components in the biosensor, the pH value was determined to be 7.38. As an important link in the generation of ECL signals, the concentration of coreactant plays an indispensable role in the size of signals. In Figure S5B, with the increasing of TEA concentration, the ECL signal gradually increases. However, the solubility of TEA in PBS is limited, so its concentration is difficult to exceed 0.5 M. Therefore, 0.5 M of TEA was selected as the optimal concentration of the coreactant. The nanoarray has excellent surface electrical activity, but excessive accumulation will increase its resistance and reduce the efficiency of the whole sensing system. Therefore, the optimal concentration of 1 mg/mL was chosen, as shown in Figure S5C display.

As shown in Fig. 4 A, the biosensor was assembled at different concentrations of cTnI. Under the optimized experimental conditions, there is an excellent linear relationship between ECL intensity and logarithm of antigen concentration, ECL intensity increases with the increase of antigen concentration in the range of 500 fg/mL to 20 ng/mL. The result is shown in Fig. 4B, and the obtained data can be fitted into a linear regression equation as y = 8419 + 2416×lg c (R2 = 0.989), and an ultralow limit of detection of 354.2 fg/mL (S/N = 3). Table S1 shows the comparison between the proposed biosensor and the ECL strategies of others in linear range and detection limit. The sensor has high sensitivity and wide detection range for detection of cTnI, indicating that the proposed biosensor has a good application prospect.

The ECL intensity of the assembled biosensor was measured for thirty-two cycles is shown in Fig. 4C. TEA (0.5 M) was used as a coreactant in PBS (pH 7.38) buffer within the voltage range of +0.6 to +1.35 V to obtain stable ECL intensity with a relative standard deviation (RSD) of 1.7 %. As shown in Fig. 4D, representative interfering proteins (1 ng/mL) CEA, PSA, PCT and NSE in human serum were used to verify the specificity of the biosensor. The ECL intensity of the biosensor was found to be very weak when the interfering proteins were tested separately. Compared to cTnI alone, the ECL intensity of the biosensor only changed slightly when the interfering proteins were mixed with cTnI separately. The reproducibility of the biosensor can be determined by measuring its signal accuracy within the same experimental group and between different experimental groups. As shown in Fig. 4E, six different ECL biosensors incubated with 1 ng/mL of cTnI in the same batch were all detected with RSD of 2.4 %. Fig. 4F shows that the same ECL biosensor incubated with 1 ng/mL of cTnI was detected in six different batches with RSD of 2.1 %. The above results show that the constructed biosensor has preeminent stability.

The spike recovery test was used to evaluate the practicability of the designed biomarker sensor for the detection of cTnI. The conventional human serum samples were obtained from Zibo Central Hospital, and the content of cTnI in the serum was detected by the invented biomarker sensor. Different concentration samples were added to the serum, respectively, then all samples were detected by the invented biosensor. The detected recovery rate was in range of 97.93–104.36%. The results were also summarized in the Table S2, compared with ELISA, the error in the detection of the same sample by this biosensor was much lower (between 1.71–7.2%). The satisfactory results of the ECL biosensor indicate that it has excellent potential in detecting cTnI.

In this work, a novel biosensor with Au NCs-GQDs quantum state complexes as the signal label and Co3O4 NAs as the sensor substrate was successfully prepared. The ECL intensity of Au NCs-GQDs is 2.5 times higher than Au NCs, and the large specific surface area and high surface electrical activity of the Co3O4 NAs improved the consistency between biosensor batches. Under optimal conditions, the biosensor has a good ECL response of cTnI with a satisfactory linear interval from 500 fg/mL to 20 ng/mL and an ultralow limit of detection of 354.2 fg/mL. The biosensor has satisfactory stability, specificity and reproducibility. Furthermore, it also has excellent detection performance in the actual sample, and it provides reference for the enhance-type sensor model.

Zhanglei Wu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Data curation, Writing - original draft. Shanghua Liu: Writing - review & editing, Investigation, Validation. Yueyuan Li: Supervision. Feng Tang: Writing - review & editing, Supervision. Zengdian Zhao: Supervision. Qing Liu: Supervision. Yueyun Li: Supervision, Validation. Qin Wei: Methodology, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by the Key Research and Development Program of Shandong Province (No. 2018GSF120001, 2018GNC110038), National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21575079). All of the authors express their deep thanks.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2021.129733.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Direct immunomagnetic detection of low abundance cardiac biomarker by aptamer DNA nanocomplex

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K.-W. Wong, D. Xu, D. He, M.S. Wong, H.-W. Li, Direct immunomagnetic detection of low abundance cardiac biomarker by aptamer DNA nanocomplex, Sensors and Actuators B: Chemical, 291(2019) 200-6.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Plasmon-induced photoreduction system allows ultrasensitive detection of disease biomarkers by silver-mediated immunoassay

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Xu, Z.H. Sun, X. Hua, H.X. Han, W. Ma, Y.T. Long, Plasmon-Induced Photoreduction System Allows Ultrasensitive Detection of Disease Biomarkers by Silver-Mediated Immunoassay, ACS Sens, 5(2020) 2184-90.
A. Venkatasubramanian, V.T. Sauer, S.K. Roy, M. Xia, D.S. Wishart, W.K. Hiebert, Nano-Optomechanical Systems for Gas Chromatography, Nano Lett, 16(2016) 6975-81.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An ultrasensitive electrochemiluminescence sensor based on reduced graphene oxide-copper sulfide composite coupled with capillary electrophoresis for determination of amlodipine besylate in mice plasma

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Wei, H. Wang, S. Sun, L. Tang, Y. Cao, B. Deng, An ultrasensitive electrochemiluminescence sensor based on reduced graphene oxide-copper sulfide composite coupled with capillary electrophoresis for determination of amlodipine besylate in mice plasma, Biosens Bioelectron, 86(2016) 714-9.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Functional analysis of an uridine diphosphate glycosyltransferase involved in the biosynthesis of polyphenolic glucoside in tea plants (Camellia sinensis)

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.M. Al-Hinaai, H.H. Kyaw, S.H. Al-Harthi, E.A. Khudaish, An innovative ECL sensor based on AuNPs linker for Ru(bpy)32+ molecules doped onto an alkaline pretreated poly(4-aminodiphenylamine) film, Sensors and Actuators B: Chemical, 257(2018) 460-8.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Zhanglei Wu: studies in school of chemistry and chemical engineering, Shandong University of Technology as postgraduate student.
Shanghua Liu: studies in school of chemistry and chemical engineering, Shandong University of Technology as postgraduate student.
Yueyuan Li: received her Ph.D. from Jinan University. Her current research focuses on electrochemical immunosensors.
Feng Tang: received his Ph.D. from Renmin University of China University. His current research focuses on electrochemical immunosensors.
Zengdian Zhao: Professor, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shandong University of Technology.
Qing Liu: received her Ph.D. in East China University of Science and Technology in 2012. Her current research focuses on electrochemical immunosensor.

Yueyun Li: received her M.S. degree in applied chemistry from Nanjing Tech University in 2002 and pH.D. degree in analytical chemistry from China University of Geosciences in 2018. Now, she is a professor at Shandong University of Technology. Her main research interests are electrochemical biosensors and photoelectrochemical biosensors and has published many articles on analysis, immunosensor and applied successfully for many research projects, such as Biosens. Bioelectron., Sens. Actuators B: Chem et al.

Qin Wei: a professor and DSc, has devoted herself to analytical teaching and scientific research. Her main research interests are the determination of protein and nucleic acid by photometry and the electrochemical immunosensor preparation. She has published over one hundred articles on analysis, immunosensor and applied successfully for many research projects, such as Adv. Mater., Biomaterials, Adv. Funct. Mater., Anal. Chem., ACS Sens., Chem. Commun., Biosens. Bioelectron., Sens. Actuators B: Chem. et al.
